waterClarity.typical: Stained to turbid, especially after rainfall events.
waterClarity.color: Brownish-green, with significant red clay runoff after heavy rains.
waterClarity.note: Water clarity is generally low due to the riverine nature and agricultural runoff in the watershed. Secchi depths can drop considerably after significant precipitation.

thermocline.note: Lake Wateree experiences moderate summer stratification, with the thermocline typically forming between 10 and 20 feet. The lake undergoes full mixing during the cooler months, characteristic of a warm monomictic or dimictic system, depending on winter severity.

oxygen.note: During summer stratification, dissolved oxygen levels can become critically low (hypoxic) below 15 feet and anoxic below 20 feet in deeper areas, particularly in the lower basin. This creates an oxygen depletion floor that limits habitat for many aquatic organisms.

limnology.waterColor: Brownish-green tint, becoming distinctly red-brown and turbid with clay runoff after heavy rainfall.
limnology.flowCharacteristics: Lake Wateree is a river-run impoundment within the Catawba-Wateree river system. It experiences significant flow-through, which influences water quality and nutrient transport. Hydroelectric power generation at the dam can create noticeable currents, particularly in the lower lake.
limnology.bottomHardness: Predominantly clay and rock, with areas of sand, gravel, and mud in coves and deeper, less scoured areas.

structuralElements.points: abundant - long tapering red clay points along the western shoreline
structuralElements.humps: several offshore humps and island tops, especially near the central basin
structuralElements.creekArms: primary creek arms include the Wateree River inlet, Little River arm, and the Catawba tributary inlet
structuralElements.channelLedges: old river channel swings create drop‑offs and ledges near the dam side
structuralElements.flats: extensive shallow flats along the northern shoreline and near the marina
structuralElements.bridges: multiple bridge pilings (US‑15/401, SC‑6) create vertical structure
structuralElements.riprap: riprap armoring present on dam spillway and bridge abutments

habitat.standingTimber: moderate - concentrated in the Little River arm and upper western coves
habitat.notes: Lake Wateree offers a diverse mix of natural and artificial structures supporting a variety of fish species; bottom is predominantly rocky and clay with moderate vegetation; structural complexity is high due to points, humps, and bridge pilings.

ramps.name: Lake Wateree State Park Boat Ramp

hazards.description: Seasonal sandbars emerge when water drops below 15 ft; marked on SC DNR chart but can shift after heavy rain

hazards.location: northwest arm near Sandy Run inlet
hazards.description: Standing timber with submerged stumps 2‑4 ft below surface; not cleared

hazards.location: immediate downstream of Lake Wateree Dam outlet
hazards.description: Hard rock ledge creates turbulent eddies; stay at least 300 ft downstream
Rapid water level changes and strong currents when generators cycle; no-wake zone 500 ft upstream

shoals: Southwest cove shoal (approx. 12‑14 ft depth at 20 ft chart datum)
Northern arm near Bear Creek (depth 10‑12 ft, exposed after drought)
standingTimberAreas: East side of the lake near Old Mill Creek where dead cypress stand 5‑10 ft below surface
bridgeHazards: US‑21 bridge (low clearance 12 ft) – restrict tall vessels during high pool
SC‑6 bridge at Lake Wateree Dam – clearance drops 2 ft when lake level rises
idleZones: Designated idle zone 300‑500 ft upstream of the dam
Marina waiting area near Lake Wateree Marina (lat 34.3612, -80.7350)
dangerousAreas: Tailwater surge zone directly below dam outlet – strong currents and hydraulic jumps
Rapid drawdown area near the southern spillway during power generation
navigation.notes: Lake Wateree generally offers moderate boating conditions, but water levels fluctuate 10‑15 ft seasonally. Check daily lake level from SC DNR before departure. Observe no‑wake zones around the dam and bridge clearances. Use depth sounder in shoal‑prone coves and keep a safe distance from standing timber, especially in low‑visibility conditions.

creelLimits.Largemouth Bass: No more than 5 combined total of smallmouth, largemouth, redeye bass or their hybrids
No more than 5 combined total of smallmouth, largemouth, redeye bass or their hybrids
No more than 5 combined total of smallmouth, largemouth, redeye bass or their hybrids

specialRegulations: A total of 40 game fish may be kept in any one day
Any game fish or species with a size limit listed must be landed with head and tail fin intact
regulations.notes: Always verify exact lake exceptions at official agency site before fishing.

striped bass.seasonalMovement: Spring migration up tributaries to spawn; post‑spawn they disperse into main lake seeking shad and herring.
striped bass.depthPreference: Shallow to mid‑water (5‑20 ft) during spawning; moves deeper (30‑80 ft) when forage concentrates near thermocline in summer.
striped bass.habitatUse: Uses the 17 SCDNR fish attractors and structure along the shoreline; follows shad schools.
striped bass.forageFocus: Threadfin shad, gizzard shad, blueback herring

largemouth bass.seasonalMovement: Relatively resident; concentrates around cover in spring and fall; moves to deeper, cooler water in summer heat.
largemouth bass.depthPreference: 5‑15 ft near cover; can be found 20‑40 ft near points and attractors during warm months.
largemouth bass.habitatUse: Utilizes fish attractors, submerged timber, and any structure; ambushes near vegetation edges.
largemouth bass.forageFocus: Juvenile shad, crappie, and catfish fingerlings

black crappie.seasonalMovement: Spreads out over flats and submerged timber in spring; seeks deeper water (15‑30 ft) in summer.
black crappie.depthPreference: 10‑25 ft near structure; often near the bottom in cooler months.
black crappie.habitatUse: Prefers the fish attractors and any submerged wood; schools near the lake’s shallower flats.
black crappie.forageFocus: Zooplankton, small insects, and baitfish larvae

catfish.seasonalMovement: Bottom‑oriented year‑round; may move to deeper channels in winter.
catfish.depthPreference: 20‑80 ft near bottom, especially around humps and creek arms if present.
catfish.habitatUse: Utilizes deep holes, humps, and any bottom structure; often near the lake’s deepest sections.
catfish.forageFocus: Bottom‑dwelling invertebrates and scavenged fish remains

striped bass.depthPreference: Shallow to mid‑water (5‑20 ft) during spawning; moves deeper (30‑80 ft) when forage concentrates near thermocline in summer.

largemouth bass.depthPreference: 5‑15 ft near cover; can be found 20‑40 ft near points and attractors during warm months.
largemouth bass.habitatUse: Utilizes fish attractors, submerged timber, and any structure; ambushes near vegetation edges.
largemouth bass.forageFocus: Juvenile shad, crappie, and catfish fingerlings

black crappie.depthPreference: 10‑25 ft near structure; often near the bottom in cooler months.
black crappie.habitatUse: Prefers the fish attractors and any submerged wood; schools near the lake’s shallower flats.
black crappie.forageFocus: Zooplankton, small insects, and baitfish larvae

catfish.seasonalMovement: Bottom‑oriented year‑round; may move to deeper channels in winter.
catfish.depthPreference: 20‑80 ft near bottom, especially around humps and creek arms if present.
catfish.habitatUse: Utilizes deep holes, humps, and any bottom structure; often near the lake’s deepest sections.
catfish.forageFocus: Bottom‑dwelling invertebrates and scavenged fish remains

summary.text:
Lake Wateree’s extensive shoreline and the 17 SCDNR‑maintained fish attractors create a productive arena for its primary predators. Striped bass, stocked regularly, follow shad migrations in spring and then hunt mid‑water schools that aggregate near the thermocline during summer. Largemouth bass remain resident, using the abundant structure and attractors to ambush prey, shifting deeper as surface temperatures rise. Black crappie and catfish round out the predator community, with crappie schooling over flats and timber while catfish patrol the deep, bottom‑laden zones.

For anglers, the key is to match the predator’s seasonal habitat: target striped bass in the shallows during spring runs and in deeper water where shad concentrate later in the season; focus largemouth bass around the attractors and any submerged timber, especially during early morning and evening cool periods; locate black crappie near the same attractors and flat vegetation in the 10‑25 ft range; and seek catfish in the lake’s deepest pockets, particularly around humps and channel ledges where bottom forage accumulates.
